content.text: Operational Readiness in Definition of Done

A GenAI feature that passes functional and security checks can still fail in production if the team is not prepared to operate it. Operational readiness is the collection of checks — runbooks, alerting, rollback procedures, on-call coverage, and capacity baselines — that ensure the feature can be observed, diagnosed, and recovered from after it ships.

Without an operational readiness gate in the Definition of Done, teams discover gaps at the worst possible moment: during an incident. This document defines the six operational readiness domains required before any GenAI feature ships to production, provides concrete checklists for each, and explains how to integrate an Operational Readiness Review (ORR) into the release process.

attrs.alt: Operational Readiness — six DoD domains with per-item checklists and gates

content.text: Why Operational Readiness Belongs in the DoD

Most Definition of Done frameworks focus on functional completeness: tests pass, code is reviewed, the feature works as designed. GenAI features add a layer of operational complexity that standard DoD frameworks do not anticipate:
Non-deterministic behaviour.
The model may produce outputs the team never explicitly tested. Runbooks must cover categories of failure, not just specific error codes.
External dependency on an API.
The Anthropic API can return 429 (rate limit), 529 (overloaded), or temporarily degrade. The feature must fail gracefully and the team must know what to do.
Cost as an operational risk.
A bug can cause runaway token consumption before anyone notices. Cost alerting is a first-class requirement, not a post-launch optimisation.
Prompt-as-code rollback complexity.
Rolling back a prompt is not the same as reverting a binary. Teams must plan for prompt version pinning and feature flag kill-switches explicitly.
Vendor SLA mismatch.
If the Anthropic API has an incident, does your on-call team know whether to wait, degrade, or escalate? That escalation path must be documented before go-live.

Operational readiness is not the last box to check — it is the prerequisite for everything else. A feature that cannot be diagnosed, recovered from, or rolled back is not done, regardless of test results.
Domain 1: Runbooks

A runbook is the on-call engineer's first stop during an incident. For GenAI features, runbooks must cover both standard API failure modes and LLM-specific failure patterns that differ from ordinary HTTP services.
Required Runbook Sections
Section
What to document
Common omission
Feature overview
What the feature does, which prompts it calls, what downstream systems it touches
No clear description of the LLM path vs other paths
Failure modes
HTTP 429 (rate limit), 529 (overloaded), 500, timeout, max_tokens truncation, empty response, JSON parse failure
Omits max_tokens truncation as a failure mode
Recovery steps
Step-by-step actions for each failure mode: wait-and-retry, degrade, escalate, roll back
Only covers 'restart the service' — no LLM-specific actions
Fallback behaviour
What the feature does when the LLM path is unavailable: static response, cached result, error to user, skip feature
No fallback defined — incidents cause total feature outage
Escalation contacts
Anthropic support URL, internal ML platform team, vendor SLA response time
Team doesn't know how to reach the vendor during an incident
Token budget exhaustion
What happens when max_tokens is reached mid-response: how is it detected, what is the user experience, is it safe to surface
Output truncation is silently passed to users
Runbook Template

## Feature: [Feature Name]

### Overview
This feature calls `[prompt_id]` (v[X.Y.Z]) to [describe purpose].
LLM path: `[API endpoint]` → `[handler]` → `[downstream system]`

### Failure Modes and Recovery

#### HTTP 429 (Rate Limited)
- Symptom: error rate spike, logs show `RateLimitError`
- Action: check Anthropic status page. Exponential backoff is automatic (4 retries).
  If sustained >5 min, enable FALLBACK_MODE flag to serve cached responses.
- Escalate to: #ml-platform if sustained >15 min

#### HTTP 529 (API Overloaded)
- Symptom: latency p95 spike, 529 errors in logs
- Action: same as 429. If >10 min, contact Anthropic support at [URL].

#### max_tokens Truncation
- Symptom: `stop_reason == "max_tokens"` in response
- Action: check if output is usable (feature-specific). If not, return
  error to caller. Do NOT surface truncated output silently.

#### JSON Parse Failure
- Symptom: `json.JSONDecodeError` in logs, feature returns 500
- Action: retry once. If fails again, return fallback response.
  Check prompt version — a recent change may have broken output format.

### Rollback
See [Rollback Procedure](#rollback-procedure).

### Contacts
- On-call: [PagerDuty rotation name]
- ML platform: #ml-platform
- Anthropic support: [support URL]
Domain 2: Alerting and Dashboards

Alerts are the automated tripwire that surfaces operational problems before users report them. GenAI features require alerts that go beyond standard HTTP error rates because the model can return a 200 response containing unusable or harmful output.
Required Alerts
Alert
Threshold
Severity
Rationale
Error rate (HTTP)
>2% over 5 min
P2
Standard API reliability signal
Latency p95
>3× baseline over 5 min
P2
LLM latency spikes indicate model load or prompt changes
Rate limit events (429)
>10 per minute
P1
Signals approaching API quota ceiling
Cost per hour
>2× 7-day average
P1
Detects runaway token consumption from bugs or abuse
Eval failure rate
>5% of sampled calls fail golden eval
P2
Catches prompt regressions that 200 responses hide
max_tokens hit rate
>10% of responses
P3
Signals prompt or max_tokens misconfiguration
Fallback activation rate
>0% (any activation)
P3
Tracks how often the LLM path is unavailable
Alert Configuration Example (Terraform / Datadog)
Dashboard Requirements
One dashboard panel per LLM feature showing: request rate, error rate, p50/p95/p99 latency, token usage (input + output), cost per day
Separate panel for stop_reason distribution (end_turn vs max_tokens vs stop_sequence) — a shift toward max_tokens is an early warning of prompt issues
Eval pass rate panel (sampled, not 100%) so prompt regressions surface in the dashboard before alerts fire
Alert test in staging before go-live: fire a synthetic 429 or force a high error rate to confirm the alert routes correctly to the on-call rotation
Domain 3: Rollback Procedure

A GenAI feature rollback is more complex than reverting a service deployment. It involves at minimum three independent artefacts: the code, the prompt template, and any configuration (model version, max_tokens, temperature). Each may need to be rolled back separately.
Rollback Components
Component
Rollback mechanism
Time to rollback
Application code
Revert commit or redeploy previous version
5–15 min (standard CD pipeline)
Prompt template
Pin PROMPT_VERSION env var to previous version, redeploy
5–10 min
Feature flag (kill-switch)
Flip flag off in feature flag service (LaunchDarkly, Split, etc.)
< 1 min
Model version
Change model field in prompt YAML, redeploy
5–15 min
Configuration (max_tokens, temperature)
Change config YAML, redeploy
5–15 min
Feature Flag Pattern for Instant Rollback
The fastest rollback mechanism is a feature flag that wraps the LLM call. When the flag is off, the feature returns a pre-defined fallback response. This does not require a code deploy.

import os
from your_flag_client import flags  # LaunchDarkly, Split, etc.

def summarise_document(document: str, user_id: str) -> str:
    if not flags.is_enabled("llm_summarise", user_id=user_id):
        # Instant rollback: flag off → serve static fallback, no LLM call
        return "Document summary is temporarily unavailable. Please try again later."

    response = anthropic_client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
        max_tokens=512,
        system=load_prompt("summarise_system", version=os.environ["PROMPT_VERSION"]),
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": document}],
    )
    return response.content[0].text
The feature flag must be tested in staging before go-live: flip it off, confirm users receive the fallback and no LLM calls are made, flip it back on, confirm normal operation resumes.
Rollback Decision Authority
Any on-call engineer may activate the kill-switch flag without approval — it is a defensive action
Rolling back the prompt version requires approval from the feature's domain reviewer or tech lead
Rolling back application code follows standard incident procedure (SRE/EM approval for production)
Target rollback time: ≤15 minutes from decision to feature disabled in production
Domain 4: On-call Readiness

An on-call engineer should not encounter a GenAI feature for the first time during an incident. On-call readiness means the engineer on duty has been briefed, has access to the runbook, knows the escalation path, and has the tools to act.
On-call Briefing Checklist
Feature overview briefing: 10-minute walkthrough of the LLM path, expected failure modes, and runbook location
Tooling access verified: on-call engineer can access feature flags, Datadog dashboard, deployment pipeline, and Anthropic support contact
Severity matrix updated: incidents involving the LLM path are classified correctly (example: high error rate → P2, data leak via LLM output → P1 immediately)
War-room channel created: #incident-[feature-name] pre-created with relevant stakeholders invited
Escalation contacts confirmed: ML platform team, EM, and vendor contacts are reachable and current
Incident Severity Matrix for GenAI Features
Severity
Condition
Initial response time
Who is paged
P1
Possible data leak via LLM output, safety filter bypass, PII in logs, >50% error rate
15 min
On-call + EM + security
P2
Error rate 2–50%, p95 latency >3× baseline, cost spike >2×, rate limit sustained
30 min
On-call + EM
P3
Eval pass rate degraded, max_tokens hit rate high, fallback activated
Next business day
On-call (acknowledge)
P4
Non-user-facing anomaly, minor config drift
Best effort
Ticket only
Domain 5: Capacity and Performance Baselines

LLM API calls are subject to provider-side rate limits and exhibit latency profiles that differ from database or cache calls. Capacity planning for a GenAI feature must account for API quota, concurrency limits, and the cost of burst traffic.
Rate Limit Headroom Calculation

# Calculate whether your Anthropic tier supports expected peak traffic
# Anthropic rate limits are expressed in requests-per-minute (RPM)
# and tokens-per-minute (TPM) per tier.

EXPECTED_REQUESTS_PER_MINUTE = 120      # from load test or traffic estimate
ANTHROPIC_RPM_LIMIT = 2000             # check your tier at console.anthropic.com
SAFETY_MARGIN = 0.7                     # use only 70% of limit (leave room for retries)

effective_limit = ANTHROPIC_RPM_LIMIT * SAFETY_MARGIN  # 1400 RPM

if EXPECTED_REQUESTS_PER_MINUTE > effective_limit:
    raise ValueError(
        f"Expected peak traffic ({EXPECTED_REQUESTS_PER_MINUTE} RPM) exceeds "
        f"safe limit ({effective_limit:.0f} RPM). Request a tier upgrade or "
        f"implement request queuing."
    )

# Token-per-minute check
AVG_TOKENS_PER_REQUEST = 1500  # input + output, from profiling
ANTHROPIC_TPM_LIMIT = 80_000

expected_tpm = EXPECTED_REQUESTS_PER_MINUTE * AVG_TOKENS_PER_REQUEST  # 180_000
if expected_tpm > ANTHROPIC_TPM_LIMIT * SAFETY_MARGIN:
    raise ValueError(f"Token budget ({expected_tpm} TPM) exceeds safe limit.")
Concurrency Cap Configuration

Without a concurrency cap, a traffic spike causes all requests to queue against the rate limit simultaneously, producing a cascade of retries that worsens the situation. Cap concurrency at the number of in-flight requests the API tier can sustain.

import asyncio
from anthropic import AsyncAnthropic

# Concurrency semaphore: at most 20 simultaneous LLM requests
# Set based on RPM limit / expected request duration
# Example: 2000 RPM / 60s = ~33 RPS; 1s avg latency → 33 concurrent → cap at 25
_sem = asyncio.Semaphore(25)

client = AsyncAnthropic()

async def call_llm_with_cap(prompt: str) -> str:
    async with _sem:
        response = await client.messages.create(
            model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
            max_tokens=1024,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        )
        return response.content[0].text
Load Test Requirements
Run at 2× expected peak traffic in staging against a mock LLM endpoint (record-and-replay, or a stub that returns realistic payloads)
Verify: p95 latency stays within SLA, error rate stays below 0.5%, concurrency cap prevents thundering-herd retries
Run at 0.5× expected traffic against the real Anthropic API (sandbox or low-traffic staging) to catch authentication, model, and rate-limit issues
Document the results: include p50/p95/p99 latency, error rate, token throughput, and cost per hour in the ORR artefact
Domain 6: Operational Readiness Review

An Operational Readiness Review (ORR) is a structured sign-off meeting or async checklist review that gates production deployment. It confirms that all five prior operational readiness domains are complete and that the people responsible for operating the feature are ready.
ORR Checklist (Async Template)

## Operational Readiness Review: [Feature Name]

**Release target date:** [date]
**Feature owner:** @[username]
**Tech lead reviewer:** @[username]
**EM sign-off:** @[username]

### Domain 1: Runbooks
- [ ] Runbook PR merged: [link]
- [ ] Failure modes reviewed by on-call lead

### Domain 2: Alerting
- [ ] All required alerts deployed (error rate, latency, cost, rate limit)
- [ ] Alert test fired and confirmed in staging
- [ ] Dashboard panel live in [dashboard link]

### Domain 3: Rollback
- [ ] Feature flag implemented and tested in staging
- [ ] Rollback procedure documented and timed (target ≤15 min)
- [ ] Rollback decision owner named: @[username]

### Domain 4: On-call Readiness
- [ ] On-call briefing completed: [date]
- [ ] Severity matrix updated
- [ ] Escalation contacts confirmed in runbook

### Domain 5: Capacity
- [ ] Rate limit headroom verified (≤70% of tier limit at peak)
- [ ] Concurrency cap configured
- [ ] Load test results attached: [link]

### Domain 6: Approvals
- [ ] Tech lead sign-off: @[username] — [date]
- [ ] EM cost approval: @[username] — [date]
- [ ] Security sign-off (if flagged): @[username] — [date]

**GO / NO-GO: ___________**
Common ORR Failures and How to Avoid Them
Failure pattern
Consequence
Prevention
Runbook written but never reviewed by on-call
On-call engineer cannot use it under pressure
Require on-call lead to acknowledge runbook before sign-off
Alerts configured but not tested
Alert fires to wrong channel or is misconfigured — discovered during a real incident
Fire a synthetic event in staging and confirm PagerDuty page fires
Feature flag implemented but not tested
Kill-switch fails to work when needed
Test: flag off → confirm fallback, flag on → confirm LLM path; document result
ORR treated as a checkbox, not a review
Sign-offs collected without reading the content
ORR owner reads runbook and alerts configuration before signing; tech lead asks at least one question in the review
On-call briefing skipped ('everyone already knows this service')
New rotation member encounters the LLM feature cold during an incident
Briefing required for any rotation member who has not been briefed on this specific feature
Load test skipped for 'low-traffic' features
Token burst from a viral sharing event hits the rate limit with no fallback
Run load test even at 2× modest traffic; the burst headroom matters more than the baseline
Integrating Operational Readiness into the Sprint Lifecycle

Operational readiness work is often deferred to the last sprint before release because it feels like overhead. This is the wrong sequencing: runbooks and alerts require understanding of failure modes that is best captured while the feature is being built.
Recommended Timing
Sprint activity
Operational readiness work to do in parallel
Feature design (Sprint 0)
Identify failure modes; add runbook skeleton to the design doc
Core LLM integration (Sprint 1)
Implement OTel spans; add feature flag wrapper from the start
Eval suite development (Sprint 1–2)
Add adversarial eval cases to inform severity matrix
Code review (Sprint 2)
Reviewer checks runbook completeness and alert configuration
Staging validation (Sprint 2)
Fire alerts in staging; run load test; test kill-switch
ORR (Sprint 2, last week)
Async sign-off; all domains checked; on-call briefed
Production deploy
Deploy behind flag at low rollout percentage; watch dashboard for 24h

If the runbook is written on the day of the production deploy, it was written too late. The best runbooks are written by engineers who still remember why each failure mode exists — that is the engineer who built it, during the sprint they built it.
Operational Readiness Anti-Patterns
Anti-pattern
Why it fails
'We'll add the runbook after launch'
The team's mental model of failure modes is sharpest during development; post-launch runbooks are written from memory and miss edge cases
Alerts copied from another service without adjustment
Thresholds and escalation paths are wrong; alerts fire too late or page the wrong team
Feature flag added only to code, not configuration
A prompt version bug cannot be killed with the flag; requires a code rollback under pressure
ORR sign-offs collected by Slack DM
No audit trail; reviewers don't read the checklist; sign-offs are not binding
'Low-traffic feature' skips load test
Traffic spikes are not predictable; rate limit headroom must be verified regardless of baseline traffic
On-call rotation updated but not briefed
Rotation member pages in but cannot find the runbook or understand the feature
Summary
Operational readiness is a first-class DoD domain for GenAI features — not an afterthought
Six domains must be complete before a GenAI feature ships: runbooks, alerting, rollback, on-call readiness, capacity baselines, and ORR sign-off
The fastest rollback mechanism is a feature flag kill-switch; it must be implemented from the start and tested before go-live
Alerts must cover GenAI-specific signals: cost spikes, rate limit events, max_tokens hit rate, and eval pass rate
The ORR is a structured sign-off that confirms all domains are complete; it must include actual review, not just checkbox collection
Operational readiness work starts in Sprint 0 alongside design — not in the last sprint before release
